evidence_quotes.quote: shielded by vague terms of service
companies in the video game industry are increasingly able, after a certain time, to revoke access to games that customers have already purchased.
Digital service providers can withdraw products at virtually any time, for any reason, as long as their terms of service mention the possibility.

paragraphs.text: Dear Members of the European Union Leadership, I am writing to draw your attention to pressing concerns regarding the disproportionate influence of payment processors over our economies and the troubling ways in which certain digital service providers mistreat and mislead consumers. Initiatives such as Stop Killing Games have highlighted an alarming trend: companies in the video game industry are increasingly able, after a certain time, to revoke access to games that customers have already purchased. This practice, while often shielded by vague terms of service, constitutes a serious abuse of power. It is reasonable that companies cannot support products indefinitely, but there must be a requirement to provide clear, fair endoflife plans. Anything less is misleading at best and deliberately deceptive at worst. This problem reflects a wider regulatory gap. Digital service providers can withdraw products at virtually any time, for any reason, as long as their terms of service mention the possibility. This gives companies sweeping control over what citizens are permitted to access, with no obligation to provide justification. If left unchecked, this sets a precedent that could expand far beyond entertainment and threaten key digital services. Equally concerning is the unchecked power of payment processors. Recent incidents involving platforms such as Steam and Itch.io show that companies like Visa and Mastercard can, seemingly without warning or due process, pressure businesses to alter policies to suit their own preferences. These decisions are often opaque yet profoundly disruptive. That a financial intermediary whose purpose should simply be to facilitate transactions can dictate how other companies operate is troubling. This influence directly affects EU citizens by undermining their freedom to spend their own money as they choose. Consumers should not be forced to accept that payment processors may decide what goods, services, or content are appropriate. That right should remain with individuals not with unaccountable financial entities. I urge you, as lawmakers and representatives, to recognize the urgency of this issue. In todays volatile and increasingly authoritarian global climate, it is critical that companies whose role is to serve consumers are not given the unchecked power to determine how we spend our time, our attention, or our resources. The internet is no longer a secondary aspect of life it is a cornerstone of modern society. It must be safeguarded so that all citizens can experience it freely, fairly, and safely. This is about far more than access to particular games or platforms. It is about preventing authoritarian practices from taking root in the digital economy and defending the principles of freedom and fair trade that underpin the European Union. Thank you for your attention to this matter. Sincerely, A concerned citizen of the EEA.
